title: Romance of the Three Kingdoms / Chapter 27 — “Beautiful Beard” Rides on a Solitary Journey / And Slays Six Men at Five Passes
text:
Of all ofCao Cao’s captains, onlyXu Huang—aside fromZhang Liao—treatedLord Guanwith genuine friendship; the rest showed him respect, with the lone exception ofCai Yang, who bore him a grudge. WhenCai Yanglearned ofGuan Yu’s departure, he was eager to pursue and capture him.Cao Cao, however, dismissed the idea. “He does not forget past favors and his conduct is always aboveboard. He is a true gentleman, and you would do well to follow his example.”

So he bade the would-be pursuers stand down and abandon any further chase.

“You treatedLord Guanwith the utmost generosity,”Cheng Yusaid, “yet he left so abruptly. Though he provided a letter of explanation, his departure still affronts Your Excellency—and that is no trivial matter. If he joinsYuan Shao, it would be like adding wings to a tiger. You had better seize and execute him to forestall future calamity.”

Cao Caoreplied, “But I gave him my word. How can I go back on it? Every man has his master; do not pursue.” But he said toZhang Liao, “He spurned every gift I offered—no bribe could move him. Such integrity commands my highest respect. He has not gone far; ride after him and plead with him to pause until I can come up and bid him farewell, offering funds for his journey and a battle robe, so that in after days he may remember me kindly.”

SoZhang Liaorode off alone.Cao Caofollowed at a leisurely pace with about twenty attendants.

NowGuan Yu’s mount was the legendaryRed Hare, famed for its unmatched speed. Under normal circumstances none could match its stride, but for the sake of the ladies’ carriage their pace was restrained. Suddenly,Lord Guanheard a shout behind him. “Slow down,Yunchang!”

He turned and recognizedZhang Liao. Ordering the pushers of the carriage to press on along the high road, he reined in his steed, heldGreen Dragonready, and stood on the bridge waiting forZhang Liao’s arrival.

“Surely you have come to escort me back,Wenyuan?” he called toZhang Liao.

“No,”Zhang Liaoreplied, “Chancellorheard you were embarking on a long journey and sent me to beg you to wait until he can come up, so that he may bid you farewell. That is the only thing.”

“Seeing that he is coming along with armored men, I must fight to the last,”Guan Yudeclared, taking a stand on the bridge as the escort approached. Four ofCao Cao’s captains followed him closely behind. Observing his readiness,Cao Caoordered his escort to open into two lines, so it might be seen that they carried no weapons. At that,Guan Yurelaxed, realizing they meant no harm.

“Why do you go in such haste,Yunchang?” askedCao Cao.

Guan Yuinclined his head but remained seated on his mount.

“I informed you in writing that with my lord is inJi province; I had no choice but to depart. I went to your palace again and again but was refused admittance. So I penned a farewell letter, sealed the treasury, surrendered my marquis seal, and entrusted everything to you. I hope Your Excellency recalls the promise you made me.”

Cao Caoreplied, “My desire is to keep my troth with all men; I cannot go back on my word. However, the road ahead is arduous, so I have prepared this bag of silver to cover your expenses.”

Then from horseback he held out a packet of gold.

Guan Yureplied, “Your previous generosity still provides me more than enough. Please reserve this for your soldiers.”

Cao Caosaid, “What reason have you to refuse? This is but a modest token that barely repays your loyalty and service.”

Guan Yureplied, “My services have been trifling—there is nothing worth mentioning.”

“Really,Yunchang,” repliedCao Cao, “you are the most high principled of men. I am very sorry my luck is too poor to retain you at my side. Pray accept just this robe as a token of my esteem.” One ofCao Cao’s captains dismounted, and presented the silk coat with both hands. Still cautious,Guan Yuremained mounted but used the tip of his blade to lift the robe, draped it over his shoulders, and bowed deeply. “I thank you, Sir Minister, for this generous gift, and I hope we shall meet again.”

With that, he dismounted from the bridge and rode off northward.

“He is a very rude man,” saidXu Zhu, who was of the escort. “Why do you not take him prisoner?”

Cao Caoreplied, “He was absolutely alone facing scores of us—his wariness was understandable. But I have given my word, and he is not to be pursued.”

Cao Caoand his escort returned; theChancellorappeared haggard and melancholy as he thought of the man who had departed.

***

But here we need say nothing more ofCao Cao’s return.Lord Guandismounted from the bridge and set off in pursuit of the carriage carrying the two ladies—which in that time should have traveled about thirty li—yet he saw no trace of it and rode back and forth searching anxiously.

Presently he heard someone shouting from a hill, calling him by name to halt. He saw a youth wearing aYellow Turbanand a silk robe, standing on horseback with a spear in hand. From the horse’s bridle dangled a bloody head, and behind him marched over a hundred foot soldiers.

“Who are you?” askedLord Guan.

The young man dropped his spear, dismounted and made a low bow.Guan Yususpected a ruse, so he checked his horse and gripped his sword more firmly, calling, “Great champion, pray tell me your name.”

“I amLiao Hua, my courtesy name isYuanjian, and I am fromXiangyang. Since these troubled times began I have been an outlaw among the rivers and lakes and I and my comrades have lived by plunder. We are about five hundred in all. By chance my friendDu Yuancame across two ladies in a carriage just now and, quite wrongly, he took them prisoners and brought them to the hold in the hills. I questioned the servants and so found out who they were and who was escorting them. So I wished them to be set free to pursue their journey.Du Yuan, drunk and rude, refused, so I slew him—and here is his head. I beg your pardon.”

“Where are the two ladies?” askedLord Guan.

“They are among the hills,” repliedLiao Hua.

“Bring them down here at once,” orderedGuan Yu.

In a short time a party of the brigands pushed the carriage down the hill, and the two ladies stood before him.

ThenLord Guandismounted, laid aside his sword and stood respectfully before them with his arms crossed.

“Sisters, have you been frightened?” he inquired.

They replied, “We should have suffered at the hands ofDu Yuanhad it not been forLiao Hua.”

“By what means didLiao Huasave you?” askedLord Guanof those who stood by.

They explained, “Du Yuanseized the two ladies and proposed that he andLiao Huaeach keep one as wife. But upon learning of their noble lineage,Liao Huasought to honor them with proper decorum. WhenDu Yuanwould not relent,Liao Huaslew him.”

Hearing thisLord Guanbowed toLiao Huaand thanked him.Liao Huathen offered to join him with his five hundred men, butLord Guan, seeing he was aYellow Turban, respectfully declined, accepting only his goodwill.Liao Huanext offered gifts, but these too were refused.

SoLiao Huatook his leave soon vanished into a valley among the hills.

Lord Guantold his sisters the story of his interview withCao Caoand the gift of a robe and then he urged the carriage on its way. As dusk fell, they reached a farmhouse, where an aged farmer came out to welcome them and asked their identity.Lord Guanreplied, “I amGuan Yu, brother ofLiu Xuande.”

“Surely you are none other thanGeneralGuan, the very man who slewYan LiangandWen Chou,” the farmer exclaimed.

“That is so,” repliedLord Guan.

“Please, come in,” said the old man, joyfully.

“My two sisters-in-law are in the carriage,” saidLord Guan. “Would you have your women of the household come out to receive them?”

AsLord Guanstood respectfully, the host urged him to sit, but he remained standing until the host’s wife returned and escorted the two ladies into the inner apartments. Only then did the host seat him in the guest hall, whereuponLord Guaninquired, “May I ask your name, Sir?”

“I amHu Hua,” he replied. “InEmperor Huan’s reign I served as apalace attendant, but later resigned and retired to my fields. I have a son,Hu Ban, who servesGrand AdministratorWang ZhiofYingyang. Are you heading that way, General? I have a letter I would like to deliver to him.”

Lord Guansaid he would take the letter. The next morning, after an early meal, the two ladies boarded the carriage, the host handed his letter toLord Guan, and the party set out once more forLuoyang.

Presently they approached a pass known asDongling Pass, guarded byKong Xiuand half a company of soldiers. When the sentries saw the carriage, they hurried to report its approach.Kong Xiurode out;Lord Guandismounted and returned his salute, andKong Xiuasked, “General, where are you bound?”

“I have leftChancellorto go intoJi provincein search of my brother,” repliedLord Guan.

“ButYuan Shaois my lord’s rival—do you have credentials from him to pass?” challengedKong Xiu.

“I departed too hastily to secure one,” repliedLord Guan.

“Without proper credentials you cannot pass. You must wait while I send for instructions,” insistedKong Xiu.

“If I wait for your report and reply, it will greatly delay me,” protestedLord Guan.

“I am bound to obey my orders—I cannot make an exception,” repliedKong Xiu.

“Then you refuse to let me pass?” askedLord Guan.

“If you wish to pass, leave your wife and daughters here as hostages,” demandedKong Xiu.

At thisLord Guanbecame enraged raised his blade to strike the officer, butKong Xiuwithdrew behind the gate and beat the drums to summon his men. Soldiers donned armor, mounted, and advanced to block the pass, shouting, “Do you dare force your way through?”

The carriage was sent off to safety, andLord GuanspurredRed Hareat full speed toward the gate commander, who planted his spear and rode out to meet him. Horses met, and with a single stroke of hisGreen DragonKong Xiu’s head fell to the ground. His soldiers scattered and fled.

“Soldiers, do not panic!” criedLord Guan. “I did not slay him out of malice but in self-defense. Report toChancellorthat this man sought my life, and I had no choice but to cut him down.”

The soldiers bowed and allowed the carriage to pass through the gates. They continued on toLuoyang. However, one guard rode swiftly ahead to inform theGrand AdministratorofLuoyangHan FuofKong Xiu’s death.Han Fusummoned his officers for counsel.CaptainMeng Tansaid, “ThisLord Guanmust be a fugitive or he would have a writ of safe passage. Our only course is to stop him or we shall incur blame.”

“The man is fierce and brave. Remember the fate ofYan LiangandWen Chou. It seems vain to oppose him by force and so we must think out some trap for him,” saidHan Fu.

“I have a ruse ready,” saidMeng Tan. “I will close the gate withthorny barriersand personally challenge him. I’ll engage then feign retreat into your ambush along the road. Once we capture him and his party and send them as prisoners toXuchang, we will be handsomely rewarded.”

This plan decided, they soon heardGuan Yuapproaching.Han Fustrung his bow, filled his quiver, and with a company of soldiers took up position beside the pass. As the party drew near,Han Fucalled out, “Who travels this way?”

Lord Guanbowed low and said, “He is a certain Guan,District MarquisofHanshou, and he wishes to pass through the checkpoint.”

“Have you a letter fromChancellor?” inquiredHan Fu.

“In the rush of departure I did not obtain one,”Lord Guanreplied.

“My special orders from theChancellorare to hold this pass and inspect every traveler in search of spies that may go to and fro. Anyone lacking credentials must be treated as a fugitive,” declaredHan Fu.

ThenLord Guan’s anger flared. He recountedKong Xiu’s fate and thundered, “Do you also seek death?”

“Who will capture him for me?” criedHan Fu.Meng Tanvolunteered, drawing his twin swords and charging atLord Guan.

Lord Guansent the carriage to safety, then spurredRed HareatMeng Tan. Meng feigned retreat toward the ambush, butRed Hare’s speed was unmatched.Guan Yuovertook him and cleaved him in two with a single stroke of hismighty sword.

The archers in the gate shot their hardest; and although a long way off, one of them lodged an arrow in his left arm. He pulled it out with his teeth, and with blood streaming down he rode toward GovernorHan Fu.Guan Yurode straight at his next victim, raised his sword, and with an oblique cut which sliced offHan Fu’s head and shoulder.

Then he drove off the remaining soldiers and returned to escort the carriage. He staunched and dressed his wound, and fearing further ambush made no long halts, pressing swiftly towardYishui Pass.

The warden ofYishui PasswasBian XiofBing province, once aYellow Turbanand now underCao Cao. His chosen weapon was a heavy iron comet hammer. Learning that the peerlessGuan Yuwas approaching, he devised an ambush: in the temple at the pass he concealed over a hundred ax-and-sword men. He meant to bidGuan Yuin for tea and, at the signal of a falling cup, unleash his hidden troops to cut him down.

All being thus arranged,Bian Xidonned plain clothes and rode out in friendly guise to meetLord Guan. WhenLord Guandismounted,Bian Xireceived him with gracious warmth.

“Guan Yu,”Bian Xibegan, “your renown shakes heaven and earth, and no warrior dares match your prowess. Your return to pay respect to yourImperial Uncleshows your loyalty and virtue.”

Guan Yuthen recounted the story of the commanders he had slain.Bian Xinodded, “Your actions were justified. When I next seeChancellor, I will explain the inner reasons for these acts.”

Believing he had found a friend,Lord Guanremounted and rode through the pass. At the temple gate, thirty monks emerged, ringing handbells in greeting.

Thistemple, dedicated to theGuardian of the State, bore the incense burners of Emperor Ming’s devotions. Inside were thirty priests—among themPujing, a countryman ofGuan Yu’s. Upon recognizing him,Pujingstepped forward to pay his respects.

“General,”Pujingsaid, “it has been many years since you leftPudong. How have you fared?”

“Yes,” saidLord Guan, “nearly twenty years.”

“Do you recognize this humble monk?”

“I left my home village so long ago that I no longer recognize you.”

“Our houses were only divided by a small stream,”Pujingsaid.

SeeingPujingspeaking of village matters,Bian Xifeared he might reveal the ambush and sternly ordered him to be silent.

“Monks prattle too much—say no more.Guan Yu, I bid you enter my hall for a feast,” saidBian Xi.

“When old villagers reunite,”Guan Yusaid, “speaking of past times is but natural.”

Pujinginvited him into the guest room for tea, butGuan Yusaid, “The two ladies in the carriage should be served first.”

Pujingordered the monks to serve tea to the two ladies, then ledLord Guaninto the inner chamber. There, he discreetly reveled a knife at his waist.Guan Yuunderstood at once and bade his men arm themselves and follow closely behind.

WhenBian Xiinvited him into theHall of the Lawfor rest,Guan Yufixed him with a steady gaze and asked, “Is this offer born of goodwill, or do you bear ill intent?”

Bian Xifroze, so taken aback that he could make no reply, and thenGuan Yuspotted armed men hidden behind the tapestries. Raising his voice,Guan Yuthundered, “What is the meaning of this? I treated you with respect—how dare you conspire against me?”

Bian Xisaw his plot undone and called to his hidden assassins to fall uponGuan Yu, butLord Guanstruck out with his short sword and scattered them at once. Their commander fled into the side chambers.Lord Guancast aside the short sword, seized hisGreen Dragon, and gave chase. WhenBian Xiraised his hammer to throw,Guan Yucut the cord and the weapon clattered to the ground. He pursuedBian Xiin and out of the chambers, caught up with him, and with one mighty stroke, cleft him in two.

After the fight,Guan Yufound the two ladies surrounded by soldiers. These fled at sight of the terrible warrior. He sought outPujingand bowed deeply: “Your timely warning saved my life. I thank you.”

“I must depart,”Pujingsaid. “This place is no longer safe.I shall pack up my few garments and my alms bowl and take to the road vague in my wanderings as the clouds in the sky. But we shall meet again—until then, take care of yourself.”

ThenLord Guantook his leave and retook the road toYongyang. The city’sGrand AdministratorwasWang Zhi,Han Fu’s kinsman by marriage. Hearing of his relative’s death, he secretly plottedLord Guan’s assassination. He stationed troops at the city gates and, when he heard thatLord Guanhad approached, rode out himself with a smiling countenance and bade him welcome.Lord Guanexplained the purpose of his journey.

“You, General, have been able to get some exercise on the road, but the ladies in their carriage must be cramped and fatigued,” beganWang Zhi. “I pray you to come into the city and all of you remain the night in the official travelers’ quarters. Tomorrow you may resume your journey.”

The offer was tempting, and his host’s earnest hospitality moved him. The two ladies entered the city and found their quarters prepared with every comfort. AlthoughLord Guanpolitely declinedGrand AdministratorWang Zhi’s invitation to a banquet, tea and provisions were sent to their lodgings.

Wearied by the day’s trials,Lord Guanbade the ladies rest first and then sat alone in the main hall—loosening his armor and seeing that his horse was well fed before settling in for the night.

Now theGrand Administratorhad asecretarynamedHu Ban, whom he entrusted with the plot against his guest. “ThisGuan Yuis a traitor toChancellorand a fugitive,” saidWang Zhi. “On the road he has slain several pass commanders and is guilty of grave crimes. But his prowess surpasses any ordinary soldier. This very night a company of a hundred torchbearers will surround his lodging and set it ablaze at midnight, leaving none alive. I will come with a force to stand by and assist if necessary.”

These orders received,Hu Banpassed them on to his men , who began secretly to prepare dry wood and other combustibles which they piled up at the gate of the rest-house.Hu Banthought within him that he would like to know what manner of man was thisLord Guan, whose fame had spread so far, so he determined to get a peep at him. He went to the rest-house and inquired whereLord Guanwas.

“The General is inside the main hall reading,” came the reply.

Hu Bannoiselessly made his way to the outside of the room and peeped in. He saw the famous warrior stroking his beard with his left hand while he read by the light of a lamp placed on a low table. An involuntary exclamation of wonder escaped at the majesty of the figure.

“Really a god!” he sighed.

“Who’s there?”Lord Guansuddenly called from within.

Hu Banentered, knelt, and announced, “Your servantHu Ban,secretaryto theGrand Administrator.”

“Surely you are the son ofHu Hua, who lives outsideXuchang?” askedGuan Yu.

“I am he,” repliedHu Ban.

ThenLord Guancalled his retainers and bade them search the baggage for the letter, which they produced. He handed it to thesecretary, who read it and sighed, “I have nearly caused the death of a righteous man.”

ThenHu Banrevealed the entire plot: “Wang Zhiis a wicked man who sought your death. Already your lodging is surrounded, and at midnight they will set it aflame. I will go and open the city gates while you hastily prepare your departure.”

Lord Guanwas taken by surprise, but he swiftly strapped on his armor, readiedRed Hare, roused the two ladies into their carriage, and rode out. Outside they found soldiers waiting with torches. The party hastened to the outskirts of the city and found the gate already open, and they lost no time in escaping the city.Hu Banreturned to give orders to fire the rest house.

They pressed on, but soon saw torches in pursuit.Wang Zhicalled out, “Halt where you are!”

Lord Guanreined in. “Worthless wretch! For what crime would you burn us alive?”

Wang Zhiwhipped up his steed and charged with spear, butLord Guancut him through with the short sword he wore at his side and scattered his followers.

Then the carriage pressed on.Lord Guan’s heart was filled with gratitude toHu Ban. When they drew nearHuazhou, someone toldLiu Yan, who rode out to greet him.Lord Guandid not dismount but bowed from horseback. “Have you been quite well since we parted?”

“Where are you bound, General?” askedLiu Yan.

“I have leftChancellorand am journeying to find my brother,”Guan Yureplied.

“Xuandeis withYuan Shao, who stands opposed toChancellor. How can you be permitted to go to him?” askedLiu Yan.

“That matter was settled long ago.”

“TheYellow Riverferry is a strategic choke-point, guarded by a lieutenant ofXiahou Dun; he will not allow you to cross.”

“Suppose then you provide boats for me?” askedLord Guan.

“Even if there are boats, I dare not lend them to you.”

“Formerly I slewYan LiangandWen Chouand saved you from a grave danger. Yet now you refuse me a ferry boat!”

“I fearXiahou Dunwould learn of it and hold me to blame.”

Lord Guanperceived that no help was to be expected from this man, so he pressed on and soon reached the ferry. There, the commander of the guard,Qin Qi, rode out to question him.

“I amGuan Yu,District MarquisofHanshou.”

“Where are you bound?” askedQin Qi.

“I journey intoJi provinceto seek my brother,Liu Xuande, and I respectfully request passage across the river.”

“Do you bear credentials fromChancellor?” askedQin Qi.

“I am not on a mission fromChancellor, so why would I carry such credentials?” repliedLord Guan.

“My orders are to guard this ferry and inspect every traveler. Without credentials, none may cross. Even if you grew wings you should not fly over.”

Lord Guan’s anger arose. “Do you know that I have been the death of all those who have hitherto tried to stop me?”

“Those you’ve slain were nameless foot soldiers—you cannot measure our relative strengths,” tauntedQin Qi. “You would not dare strike me down.”

Guan Yuretorted, “You think yourself an equal toYan LiangorWen Chou?”

Enraged,Qin Qispurred forward with sword in hand. Their mounts met and with a single, mighty swing ofGreen Dragon,Lord Guancleaved offQin Qi’s head.

“He who opposed me lies dead—none of you need fear,” calledLord Guanout to the soldiers. “Make haste and prepare a ferry!”

A boat soon ranged alongside. The two ladies embarked first, andLord Guanfollowed. They crossed, and were then in the country ofYuan Shao. In the course of his journey to this pointLord Guanhad forced through five passes and slain six captains.

His seal he laid aside, the treasury’s doors were barred with care,

From gilded halls he rode, to seek the brother lost to years.

Red Harethundered swift as storm-winds swept the plain,

Green Dragoncleft iron gates and rent their chains.

His loyalty and truth shone bright, a beacon to the bold,

His valor could still raging streams and make high mountainfold.

Alone he pressed unflinching—death itself to meet his blade,

Sung by countless voices, his glory shall not fade.

“I did not willingly slay a single one of them,” musedLord Guanas he rode. “There was no help for it. Nevertheless whenCao Caohears of this, he will regard me as ungrateful for his bounty.”

Before long he saw a rider on the road who soon hailed him and proved to beSun Qian.

“I have never heard a word from you since we lost sight of each other atRunan; how have you fared?” askedLord Guan.

“After your departure the city fell. I was sent toYuan Shaoto try to make peace with him and succeeded, so that he invited your brother to go to him and share in the deliberations for an attack onCao Cao. But to my disgust the leaders ofYuan Shao’s army showed great jealousy of each other so that one got into jail, another was degraded, and others quarreled. MeanwhileYuan Shaovacillated and hesitated, so that your brother and I consulted how we might remove ourselves. Now theImperial Uncleis atRunanwithLiu Piand, thinking you could not know that and might suffer some harm if you unwillingly went toYuan Shao, I have come to warn you. It is good fortune to find you like this. Now we can hasten toRunanand you will meet your brother.”

Lord GuantookSun Qianto make his bow to the ladies, who asked after his adventures and he told them of the risksXuandehad run fromYuan Shao’s sudden bursts of anger. Now, however, he was out of his way and safe atRunanwhere they would meet him.

They covered their faces and wept at the recital of his dangers. Then the party no longer traveled north but took the road towardRunan. Not long after a great cloud of dust was noticed behind them and they presently made out a squadron of horsemen. These were led byXiahou Dun, who shouted out toLord Guanto stop.

One by one the pass commanders stopped his progress and were slain,

The river crossed, another army comes and he must fight again.

How finallyLord Guanescaped death will appear in the next chapter.
